concept_name: A Bar Magnet in a Uniform Field (τ = m × B, U = −m·B, T = 2π√(I/mB))
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics Part 1, Chapter 5 (Magnetism and Matter) §5.2.3 (The dipole in a uniform magnetic field) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the universal real-world anchor (a magnetic compass needle and a lab vibration magnetometer) — Rule 35: no country-specific culture anywhere in the sim. The atomic concept taught here is what a UNIFORM external field B does to a bar magnet of magnetic moment m: each pole feels a force (the N pole +mB along B, the S pole −mB opposite), and because these forces are equal, opposite, and act on different lines they form a COUPLE — the net force is ZERO (the magnet does not translate) but the couple produces a torque τ = m × B of magnitude mB·sinθ that rotates the magnet toward alignment with B. The torque is maximum at θ = 90° (a cross product is largest when the vectors are perpendicular) and zero at θ = 0° and θ = 180°. Released near alignment the magnet OSCILLATES about θ = 0° with period T = 2π√(I/mB), where I is its moment of inertia — the principle of the vibration magnetometer. The potential energy is U = −m·B = −mB·cosθ: a MINIMUM at θ = 0° (stable equilibrium — a nudge produces a restoring torque) and a MAXIMUM at θ = 180° (unstable — any nudge grows and the magnet flips to θ = 0°). Five student misconceptions are confronted, each at its own pivot state via a straightforward cause-then-effect contrast beat (Rule 16a / Rule 31 — never a predict-pause, never wait_for_answer): (M2) the magnet feels no force at all (each pole DOES feel mB; they form a couple) — STATE_3; (M1) a uniform field pushes the magnet bodily along B (net force is zero — only a torque) — STATE_4, the PRIMARY aha; (M3) torque is maximum when m ∥ B (it is zero there; maximum at 90°) — STATE_6; (M4) θ = 180° is as stable as θ = 0° (it is the energy maximum — unstable) — STATE_7; (M5) the oscillation period is independent of the field (T = 2π√(I/mB), so a stronger m or B gives a faster swing) — STATE_8. This is the magnetic twin of electric_dipole_in_field (τ = p × E, U = −p·E), reusing its rotational-dynamics renderer. CUT-LINE: the bar magnet's own field / closed-loop lines / no-monopole / axial-equatorial 1/r³ is the prerequisite bar_magnet_as_dipole; Gauss's law for magnetism (∮B·dA = 0) is the downstream gauss_law_magnetism. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides, Rule 20). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; the five misconceptions are confronted inside EPIC-L via straightforward misconception_watch contrast beats, not predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the magnetic compass needle from your geography or physics class. The needle is a tiny bar magnet sitting in the Earth's uniform field. Let it go and it swings until it points along the field, then holds there; push it aside and it springs straight back. That 'springs back to alignment' is exactly the torque τ = mB·sinθ doing its job — and a bar magnet in any uniform field behaves the very same way.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the vibration magnetometer used in the school lab — a small bar magnet hung by a thread that you twist and release, then count how many swings it makes per minute. The time for one swing is the period T = 2π√(I/mB); a stronger magnet or a stronger field makes it swing faster. That timed swing is how the lab actually measures a magnetic field.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture a bar magnet floating on a cork in a bowl of water, free to turn. It rotates until its N pole points roughly along the field and settles there — the same τ = m × B settling the magnet into its lowest-energy pose.

m.name: Magnetic moment of the bar magnet

B.name: Magnitude of the uniform external field

I.name: Moment of inertia of the magnet

formulas.torque_vector: τ = m × B — the vector torque is the cross product of the magnetic moment with the field; it is perpendicular to both m and B.
formulas.torque_magnitude: τ = mB·sinθ — scalar magnitude; θ is the angle between m and B; maximum at θ = 90°, zero at θ = 0° or 180°.
formulas.net_force_zero: F_net = (+mB) + (−mB) = 0 in a UNIFORM field — the two pole-forces are equal and opposite, so the magnet rotates but does not translate.
formulas.potential_energy: U = −m·B = −mB·cosθ — minimum (−mB) at θ = 0°, zero at θ = 90°, maximum (+mB) at θ = 180°.
formulas.oscillation_period: T = 2π√(I/mB) — released near alignment the magnet oscillates about θ = 0° with this period (I = moment of inertia); a stronger m or B gives a faster swing.
formulas.equilibrium_stable: θ = 0° (m ∥ B) — stable equilibrium; U is a minimum, and a small nudge produces a restoring torque toward alignment.
formulas.equilibrium_unstable: θ = 180° (m anti-parallel to B) — unstable equilibrium; U is a maximum, and any tiny nudge grows until the magnet flips to θ = 0°.

constraints: B must be uniform across the magnet — a non-uniform field adds a net force (gradient term) so the magnet would also translate; this concept only covers the uniform case.
The magnet is rigid — its magnetic moment m and length are fixed during rotation.
τ = m × B is a vector perpendicular to BOTH m and B; its magnitude is mB·sinθ, and ΣF = 0 exactly, for every θ, in a uniform field.
θ is measured from m to B; a small positive θ near 0° gives a restoring torque back toward θ = 0° (stable); a small departure from θ = 180° gives a torque that GROWS away from it (unstable).
U = −m·B is defined up to an additive constant; the reference used throughout is U = 0 at θ = 90°, so U ranges from −mB (θ=0°, minimum) to +mB (θ=180°, maximum).
The small-oscillation period T = 2π√(I/mB) holds for small amplitudes about the stable equilibrium θ = 0° — STATE_8 uses a ±25° amplitude, within the small-angle regime NCERT scope assumes.

B is expressed on a ×10⁻⁴ T scale for slider range; every torque/energy/period formula must multiply the raw slider value by 1e-4 before use — never compute with the raw slider integer as if it were Tesla.

aha_moment.statement: Net force is zero, yet the torque is not — the magnet turns without moving.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: Watch STATE_3 → STATE_4: the two equal-and-opposite pole forces resolve to a ΣF = 0 badge, yet the τ vector appears and the magnet begins to turn. Both facts are true at once.

STATE_1.title: A bar magnet carries a moment m, S → N

scene_composition.text: A bar magnet: N (red), S (blue), and its moment m

The magnet holds its home pose. Then the yellow m
arrow grows out from S to N.

Its length shows how strong the magnet is.
No external field yet.
A compass needle is exactly this — a tiny bar magnet with moment m

tts_sentences.text_en: This is a bar magnet — north pole red, south pole blue.
Its magnetic moment m, in yellow, points from south to north and measures how strong it is.

STATE_2.title: A uniform field B — the same everywhere

scene_composition.text: A uniform field B — identical everywhere

Blue field arrows sweep in, all the same
length and direction.

The magnet sits tilted at angle θ. The yellow
arc marks that angle to B.
Uniform means the field never changes strength or direction, anywhere

tts_sentences.text_en: Now switch on a uniform magnetic field B — every blue arrow is identical, same length and direction everywhere.
The magnet sits tilted at angle theta to the field, marked by the yellow arc.

STATE_3.title: Each pole feels mB — a couple

scene_composition.text: Each pole feels a force: +mB and −mB

The field shimmers once. Then the north pole's
+mB arrow grows out along B.

The south pole's −mB arrow grows out against
B — equal, opposite, different lines.
That pairing is a couple, not a cancellation — the magnet still feels forces

tts_sentences.text_en: The field shimmers once, then each pole answers separately.
The north pole feels a force m B along the field; the south pole feels the same size force, m B, but directed against it.
Equal, opposite, on different lines — that pairing is a couple, not a cancellation.

misconception_watch.belief: A magnet placed in a uniform field feels no force at all, because the two poles are equal and opposite.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The north pole's +mB arrow grows out along the field, then the south pole's −mB arrow grows out against it — two real forces, equal and opposite, arranged on different lines to form a couple.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Each pole DOES feel a force mB. The two are equal and opposite, so they form a couple — zero net force, but a real twisting torque.

STATE_4.title: Net force zero — it still turns

scene_composition.text: ΣF = 0, yet the magnet turns

The two force arrows pulse, then sum to zero —
a badge confirms it, centre fixed.

The couple still makes a torque τ (purple).
The magnet swings toward B.
Zero net force and non-zero torque are both true at the same instant

tts_sentences.text_en: Add the two pole forces: plus m B and minus m B cancel, so the net force is zero — a badge confirms it.
The couple still creates a torque tau, the purple arrow, and the magnet swings toward the field, centre pinned.
Zero force, non-zero torque — both true at once.

misconception_watch.belief: A uniform field pushes the magnet bodily along the direction of the field.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The centre-pin holds fixed and the ΣF = 0 badge stamps while only the magnet's orientation changes — it turns in place, it never slides.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: In a UNIFORM field the net force is exactly zero — the magnet only rotates to align with B, it does not translate.

drill_downs: if forces cancel how does it turn
what exactly is a couple in physics
would a magnet slide in a field that is not uniform
why do the two mB forces not cancel completely

STATE_5.title: τ = mB·sinθ — a sine in the angle

scene_composition.text: τ = mB·sinθ — watch it rise and fall

θ sweeps smoothly from 0° to 180°
and back.

The purple τ arrow grows, peaks at the
middle, then shrinks again.
That shape is a sine curve: τ = mB·sinθ

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the angle theta sweep smoothly from zero to one eighty degrees and back.
The torque arrow tau grows, peaks at the middle, then shrinks again — tracing a sine curve, tau equals m B sine theta.

STATE_6.title: τ max at 90°, zero at 0° and 180°

scene_composition.text: Biggest at 90°, zero at 0° and 180°

The magnet snaps to 0° — the τ arrow
vanishes.

It snaps to 90° — τ fully extends,
τ = mB, the maximum.
At 180° it vanishes again — a cross product peaks ⊥, dies ∥

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnet snaps to three poses.
At zero degrees, aligned with the field, the torque arrow vanishes.
At ninety degrees it fully extends — tau equals m B, the maximum.
At one eighty it vanishes again: a cross product peaks when perpendicular, dies when parallel.

misconception_watch.belief: Torque is maximum when the moment m is parallel to the field B, because that is when the magnet is most aligned.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: At the 0° and 180° held poses the τ arrow disappears entirely; at the 90° pose it snaps out fully extended, with the HUD reading τ = mB.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Alignment means τ = 0, not τₘₐₓ. τ = m × B is a cross product — it is largest when m ⊥ B (θ = 90°).

drill_downs: why is torque biggest at 90 degrees not 0
why is torque zero when magnet is aligned with field
which way does torque point using right hand rule
how is torque related to cross product geometry

STATE_7.title: 0° springs back; 180° flips away

scene_composition.text: Nudge: back vs flip

Nudged from 0°, the magnet springs straight
back — U sits at its minimum.

Nudged the same amount from 180°, it flips
all the way to 0° instead.
Same small nudge, opposite fate: one stable, one unstable

tts_sentences.text_en: Nudge the magnet slightly from zero degrees — it springs right back, and the U meter sits at the bottom, its energy minimum.
Nudge it the same amount from one eighty — the deviation grows instead, and it flips to zero as the U marker falls from top to bottom.
Same nudge, opposite fate.

misconception_watch.belief: θ = 180° is just as stable as θ = 0°, because the torque is zero at both angles.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The same small nudge is applied at both poses: at 0° the magnet springs straight back and the U-marker sits at the bottom; at 180° the deviation grows instead, and the magnet flips all the way to 0° as the U-marker falls from the top.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Only θ = 0° is stable — the energy minimum. θ = 180° is the energy maximum, so any nudge grows and the magnet flips to θ = 0°.

drill_downs: why is 180 degrees not stable if torque is zero there too
why does U have a negative sign in the formula
how much work does it take to flip the magnet from 0 to 180
what makes one equilibrium stable and another unstable

scene_composition.text: Stronger field, faster swing

The magnet oscillates steadily about 0°;
the period T reads on the HUD.

The field steps up — B doubles, the
arrows lengthen and brighten.
The swing quickens at once and T drops, exactly as T = 2π√(I/mB) predicts

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnet oscillates steadily about zero degrees; T ticks on the readout.
Now the field steps up — B doubling, arrows lengthening and brightening.
Watch the swing quicken at once, and T on the readout drop.
Exactly as T equals two pi root I over m B predicts — a stronger field, a faster swing.

misconception_watch.belief: The oscillation period of the magnet does not depend on the magnet or the field.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Partway through the steady swing, B visibly steps up — the field arrows lengthen and brighten — and the swing immediately quickens while the T readout on screen drops.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: T = 2π√(I/mB): a stronger moment m or a stronger field B makes the swing FASTER (shorter period); a heavier magnet (larger I) makes it slower.

STATE_9.title: Your turn — drag m, B and θ

scene_composition.text: Explore: drag m, B, and θ

Watch τ, U, and the period T respond
live to every drag.

Try θ = 90° for maximum τ. Try θ = 0°
for the lowest U.
τ = mB·sinθ · U = −mB·cosθ · T = 2π√(I/mB)

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn — drag m, B, and theta, and watch torque tau, energy U, and period T respond live.

regeneration_variants.label: The compass needle
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A magnetic compass needle resting in the Earth's roughly uniform field
regeneration_variants.same_physics: The needle IS a tiny bar magnet of moment m; the couple ±mB gives it a torque τ = m × B that swings it into alignment and springs it back from a nudge, exactly as in this concept
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A pivoted compass needle tilted at angle θ to the Earth's field lines, swinging toward alignment and springing back when nudged.

regeneration_variants.label: The lab vibration magnetometer
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A small bar magnet suspended by a thread in the school lab, twisted and released
regeneration_variants.same_physics: The timed swing about θ = 0° has period T = 2π√(I/mB) — the same oscillation this concept teaches, used here to MEASURE an unknown field
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A thread-suspended bar magnet oscillating gently about the field direction, with a stopwatch counting swings.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can explain that a bar magnet of moment m in a UNIFORM field B feels equal and opposite pole forces (+mB on N, −mB on S) that form a couple — zero net force, so the magnet does not translate — but a torque τ = m × B of magnitude mB·sinθ that rotates it toward alignment; that the torque is maximum at θ = 90° (a cross product is largest when the vectors are perpendicular) and zero at θ = 0° and 180°; that released near alignment it oscillates about θ = 0° with period T = 2π√(I/mB), so a stronger m or B makes it swing faster; and that the potential energy U = −m·B = −mB·cosθ is a minimum at θ = 0° (stable) and a maximum at θ = 180° (unstable), so the magnet always settles pointing along the field.

questions.stem: A bar magnet is placed in a uniform magnetic field. What does the field do to its two poles?

options.A: Each pole feels a force mB — +mB on N along B, −mB on S against B — equal and opposite on different lines, forming a couple
options.B: The poles feel no force, because they are equal and opposite
options.C: Both poles feel a force in the same direction, pushing the magnet bodily along B
options.D: Only the north pole feels a force; the south pole feels nothing

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks equal-and-opposite poles means no force at all, missing that each pole feels mB and they form a couple
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the two pole forces add to a net force that translates the magnet (they are opposite, so they cancel)
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks only one pole interacts with the field

questions.tested_idea: Each pole feels a force mB; equal and opposite on different lines → a couple (ΣF = 0).
questions.parallel_form_stem: A compass needle sits in a uniform field. Describe the force on each of its poles and explain why the two forces form a couple rather than pushing it sideways.

questions.stem: In a uniform field the net force on a bar magnet is zero. Does that mean nothing happens to it?

options.A: No — the couple still produces a torque τ = m × B, so the magnet rotates toward alignment without translating
options.B: Yes — zero net force means the magnet stays completely still
options.C: No — the magnet slides along the field because the forces add up
options.D: Yes — a uniform field can never affect a magnet

distractor_misconceptions.B: Conflates zero net force with zero torque — misses that a couple turns the magnet
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the pole forces produce translation (they cancel; only the torque acts)
distractor_misconceptions.D: Denies that a uniform field exerts any effect

questions.tested_idea: Zero net force but non-zero torque: the magnet rotates without translating.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A bar magnet released at an angle in a uniform field does not drift across the room. Explain what it does instead and why, in terms of force and torque.

questions.stem: At what angle between m and B is the torque on the magnet largest, and what is its value there?

options.A: At θ = 90°, where τ = mB·sin 90° = mB
options.B: At θ = 0°, where m is aligned with B
options.C: At θ = 180°, where m points opposite to B
options.D: The torque is the same at every angle

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks maximum torque is at alignment, where in fact τ = 0
distractor_misconceptions.C: Picks anti-alignment, where τ is also zero

questions.tested_idea: τ = mB·sinθ is maximum at θ = 90°, zero at 0° and 180°.
questions.parallel_form_stem: As a magnet turns from aligned to perpendicular in a uniform field, describe how the torque on it changes and state the angle of maximum torque.

questions.stem: Why is the torque on the magnet zero when its moment m is parallel to the field B?

options.A: Because τ = m × B is a cross product, which is zero when the two vectors are parallel (sin 0 = 0)
options.B: Because the field stops existing when the magnet is aligned
options.C: Because the pole forces become zero at alignment
options.D: Because the magnet's moment becomes zero at alignment

distractor_misconceptions.B: Invents a vanishing field rather than the sinθ factor
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the pole forces vanish (they are still mB; their lines of action coincide so there is no couple)
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks the moment vanishes at alignment

questions.tested_idea: τ = m × B is a cross product → zero when m ∥ B (sin 0 = 0).
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain, using the cross product τ = m × B, why a magnet already lined up with the field feels no turning effect.

questions.stem: A magnet rests aligned with the field (θ = 0°) and another rests anti-aligned (θ = 180°). How do these two equilibria differ?

options.A: θ = 0° is stable (U minimum, a nudge restores it); θ = 180° is unstable (U maximum, a nudge flips it to 0°)
options.B: Both are equally stable, since the torque is zero at both
options.C: θ = 180° is stable and θ = 0° is unstable
options.D: Neither is an equilibrium, because the field keeps turning the magnet

distractor_misconceptions.B: Equates zero torque with stability, ignoring the energy criterion
distractor_misconceptions.D: Denies that aligned/anti-aligned are equilibria at all

questions.tested_idea: U = −mB·cosθ: θ = 0° is the stable minimum, θ = 180° the unstable maximum.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Using the energy U = −mB·cosθ, explain which of the two zero-torque orientations a freely suspended magnet will actually settle into, and why.

questions.stem: A suspended bar magnet oscillates about the field direction with period T = 2π√(I/mB). If you replace it with a stronger magnet (larger m) in a stronger field (larger B), what happens to the period?

options.A: The period gets shorter — it swings faster, because T ∝ 1/√(mB)
options.B: The period gets longer — it swings slower
options.C: The period does not change; it is independent of m and B

distractor_misconceptions.B: Gets the dependence backwards (more mB → faster, not slower)
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks the period is independent of the magnet and field
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks a stronger field halts oscillation rather than speeding it

questions.tested_idea: T = 2π√(I/mB): a stronger m or B shortens the period (faster swing).
questions.parallel_form_stem: In a vibration magnetometer the magnet swings faster in a stronger field. Justify this from the period formula T = 2π√(I/mB).
